content.text: allow you to run Claude Code directly inside your editor (VS Code, JetBrains IDEs, Cursor) instead of the terminal. IDE integration is
—all Claude Code features work from the terminal alone, but extensions add convenience like one-click launch and side-by-side code/AI views.

The IDE integration solves a common problem: instead of switching to a separate terminal to get Claude's help, you can launch it with your current context already shared—current file, selected code, and error messages are visible automatically.
(beta) brings Claude Code directly into your IDE with a richer, graphical experience.
After installation, launch Claude Code multiple ways:
Click the Spark (✱) icon in the top-right when a file is open
Click the Claude icon → choose a session or start new
in the bottom-right (even with no file open)
The extension is a GUI wrapper for the CLI, adding these features:
When Claude edits a file, compare original vs changes side-by-side; accept/reject
In Plan Mode, open Claude's plan as a markdown doc and provide inline comments
Search past sessions by keyword or browse by time in panel dropdown
to enable/disable servers and manage OAuth authentication
Toggle focus between editor and Claude
opens Task Manager, so these shortcuts
Click the Claude Code icon in the
(Windows/Linux) to insert a @reference with file path and line numbers into your prompt.

- Current file you're editing
- Text selection
- Error messages from VS Code Problems panel
- Workspace file structure
What you don't need to do:

- Copy-paste code snippets
- Explain file locations
- Describe error messages manually
Claude Code works fully from the terminal without any IDE. This is especially useful for server environments or SSH sessions:
Claude uses the current directory as its workspace.
Install Claude Code extension in Cursor
— all features work from terminal alone
: Install the official Claude Code extension
: inline diff, @file references, Plan review, multi-tab sessions
Understanding the internals of ide extensions helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Production experience with ide extensions has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Deploying ide extensions in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.

Following best practices ensures effective use of IDE Extensions in real-world applications.
Start with the simplest approach that solves your problem before adding complexity.
Test edge cases thoroughly, especially boundary conditions and error states.
Document your implementation decisions for future maintainability.
Monitor performance metrics and optimize based on actual usage patterns.
Follow security best practices and validate all inputs at system boundaries.
Use version control and code reviews for all production changes.
Establish clear error handling strategies before deployment.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Understanding typical pitfalls in IDE Extensions helps you avoid costly errors.
Over-engineering solutions when simpler approaches would suffice — start minimal.
Neglecting error handling, leading to silent failures in production.
Skipping testing because the implementation appears straightforward.
Ignoring rate limits and quotas when designing at scale.
Not considering the user experience impact of latency or error states.
Optimizing IDE Extensions for production environments requires attention to key performance factors.
Implement request batching and connection pooling
Monitor token usage and optimize prompt length
Security is paramount when implementing IDE Extensions in production systems.
Never expose API keys in client-side code or public repositories.
Validate and sanitize all user inputs before processing.
Implement proper authentication and authorization controls.
Audit logs for all sensitive operations and data access.
Follow the principle of least privilege for all service accounts.

This section covered IDE Extensions in depth, including core concepts, practical implementation approaches, and production considerations. Apply these patterns consistently to build robust, maintainable systems.
VS Code Extension: Installation and Setup

The official Claude Code VS Code extension (publisher: Anthropic) integrates Claude Code into VS Code. It wraps the Claude Code CLI — the CLI must be installed first, and the extension communicates with it via local process. This architecture means all Claude Code capabilities (file editing, bash, MCP tools) are available inside VS Code, with the added benefit that the extension can see your current file and selected code automatically.

# Installing Claude Code VS Code Extension

# Step 1: Install Claude Code CLI (the extension wraps the CLI)
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code

# Step 2: Authenticate
claude login          # Opens browser for OAuth
# Or use API key directly:
# export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..."

# Step 3: Verify CLI works
claude --version      # Should show version number
claude "Hello"        # Quick sanity check

# Step 4: Install the VS Code extension
# Option A: VS Code UI
# Extensions panel (Ctrl+Shift+X) -> Search "Claude Code" (publisher: Anthropic) -> Install

# Option B: Command line
code --install-extension anthropic.claude-code

# Step 5: Open a project in VS Code
# The Claude Code icon appears in the Activity Bar (left sidebar)
# Click it to open the chat panel

The extension reads project-level configuration from .vscode/settings.json and respects .claudeignore files (same gitignore syntax) in your project root. Keyboard shortcuts are fully customizable. The most impactful setting for large codebases is excludePatterns — excluding node_modules and build artifacts dramatically reduces the context Claude needs to process.

JetBrains IDEs (IntelliJ, PyCharm, WebStorm, GoLand) support Claude Code through the official JetBrains Marketplace plugin. The plugin architecture mirrors VS Code: it wraps the Claude Code CLI in a JetBrains tool window. JetBrains-specific integration includes the built-in VCS diff viewer for reviewing changes and IDE inspection results as additional context (equivalent to VS Code diagnostics).

Vim/Neovim users can integrate Claude via the community avante.nvim plugin, which provides a split-pane interface with Claude using tree-sitter for code-aware context. Avante supports the same Anthropic API models as Claude Code and provides diff-mode for reviewing changes. Simpler workflows can just run claude in a :terminal split without any additional plugins.
IDE Integration vs Pure CLI: When to Use Each

Both give access to identical Claude Code capabilities, but context transfer differs in ways that matter. In the IDE extension, your current file and diagnostics are sent automatically with every message — passive context. In the terminal, you describe what you are working on explicitly or let Claude read files via tools. For editing-focused tasks, the IDE experience is smoother; for automation and scripting, the CLI is more powerful.
: Passive file context, inline diff review, diagnostics integration, sidebar persistence, editor keyboard shortcuts
: Works in any environment (SSH, Docker, CI), no IDE dependency, scriptable, better for non-code tasks and full-project refactors
: Bug fixing with error context, focused code editing, code review sessions in the editor, pair programming
: Server environments, CI automation, tasks spanning many files, full codebase refactoring, non-GUI workflows
: Many developers run both — extension for current-file work, terminal for broader tasks — sharing the same Claude Code session

IDE extensions run with the same OS permissions as the IDE itself. The Claude Code extension does not make direct API calls — it communicates with the CLI process, which makes the API calls. Your API key is stored in ~/.claude/settings.json by the CLI, not in VS Code settings (which can sync to the cloud). File contents sent to Claude are transmitted to Anthropic's API over HTTPS — functionally identical to what happens when using the CLI.
: Stored in ~/.claude/settings.json by the CLI, not in VS Code extension settings where Settings Sync could upload it
: File contents go to Anthropic's API over HTTPS — same data flow as the terminal Claude Code
: Requests read access to workspace files and write access to apply edits — review during installation
: Never store API keys in .vscode/settings.json — VS Code may sync these to the cloud
: VS Code's Workspace Trust feature can restrict extension capabilities in untrusted folders
: The extension needs the claude binary on PATH. If using nvm or a version manager, ensure the active Node version's bin is on PATH in the IDE's environment.
: Keep extension and CLI versions in sync. Newer CLI with older extension (or vice versa) can cause protocol errors.
: Use .claudeignore to exclude generated files, node_modules, and build artifacts. Without it, Claude gets unnecessary context that wastes tokens.
: Claude's diff view in VS Code is a tab. Closing it without accepting/rejecting loses the changes — always resolve diffs before closing.
: GitHub Copilot, Cursor, and Claude Code can conflict on inline suggestion triggers. Configure non-overlapping keybindings.
